verdict_reasoning: ABCB9 (TAPL) is a multi-pass ABC transporter that resides on lysosomal/late-endosomal membranes, not the plasma membrane. Its topology places the substrate-translocating face toward the lysosomal lumen, transporting peptides from cytosol into lysosomes. Checking contextual buckets: (1) cell_state_induced — no documented stress- or activation-induced PM redistribution; (2) tissue_restricted_surface — no lineage-specific PM display reported; (3) lysosomal_exocytosis — while lysosomal TM proteins can reach PM via exocytosis, no peer-reviewed surface biotinylation, flow cytometry on intact cells, or surface proteomics data documents ABCB9 at the PM via this route; (4) dual_localization — no PM pool described; (5) stable_surface_attachment — it is not secreted and not known to attach to a PM partner. No therapeutic antibody/ADC/CAR program targeting ABCB9 on the cell surface is documented. ABCB9 is an endomembrane (lysosomal) resident transporter.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Lysosomal exocytosis theoretically could deliver ABCB9 transiently to the PM, but no experimental evidence (surface flow cytometry, biotinylation) confirms this for ABCB9 specifically.

Triage the human gene **ABCB9**.

Canonical identifiers and gene summary (machine-resolved from HGNC and NCBI; no further lookups available — judge from the context below plus your trained knowledge):

- HGNC approved name: ATP binding cassette subfamily B member 9
- HGNC symbol: ABCB9
- UniProt accession: Q9NP78
- Aliases: EST122234, TAPL
- Previous symbols: (none)
- HGNC gene-group memberships: ATP binding cassette subfamily B
- CD nomenclature: (none assigned)
- NCBI summary: The membrane-associated protein encoded by this gene is a member of the superfamily of ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters. ABC proteins transport various molecules across extra- and intra-cellular membranes. ABC genes are divided into seven distinct subfamilies (ABC1, MDR/TAP, MRP, ALD, OABP, GCN20, White). This protein is a member of the MDR/TAP subfamily. Members of the MDR/TAP subfamily are involved in multidrug resistance as well as antigen presentation.
